summary:
Obsidian — a naturally occurring volcanic glass that fractures to produce exceptionally sharp cutting edges — was among the most valued materials in the prehistoric world and one of the first commodities to be exchanged over long distances. Sources in central Anatolia, particularly around the volcanic region of Cappadocia (sites such as Ciftlik and Acigol), and in eastern Anatolia near Lake Van, supplied obsidian that has been found at Neolithic sites hundreds of kilometers distant, demonstrating organized trade and exchange networks active by at least 9500 BCE.

At sites like [[event:2|Jericho]] and [[event:1304|Pre-Pottery Neolithic A]] settlements throughout the Levant, obsidian artifacts from Anatolian sources appear alongside local flint tools, confirming that even the earliest permanent settlements participated in inter-regional exchange. The [[event:1387|obsidian long-distance trade]] networks extended from Anatolia into the Levant, Cyprus, and eventually much of the Near East. Obsidian sourcing through geochemical analysis has become a key tool for archaeologists mapping prehistoric contact and exchange patterns.
